Sample Digest · Monday 27 April 2026

A recent Obstetrics & Gynaecology digest,
as sent to subscribers

This is the real Monday digest from Monday 27 April 2026. Showing the first 8 of 17 articles from that week — every new paper from the top journals in Obstetrics & Gynaecology.

17articles that week
4journals covered
£0for your first 4 weeks
Note: Summaries are AI-generated from publicly available PubMed abstracts. Always verify against the source article before clinical use.
American journal of obstetrics and gynecology

Reducing the long-term impact of cesarean scar defects: a focus on prevention

Walker SP, Bartels HC, Jurkovic D et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Narrative review
Key Question: How can optimized cesarean section surgical techniques reduce the formation and long-term complications of cesarean scar defects?
Key Findings:
  • Emerging evidence suggests unlocked, interrupted, purse-string, and endometrium-free sutures with monofilament or barbed materials may reduce scar formation compared to continuous locked techniques
  • Surgical repair of existing scar defects improves chronic gynecological symptoms and subfertility outcomes
  • Evidence for preventing obstetric complications through scar repair remains limited, with theoretical concerns about increasing dehiscence/rupture risk
Clinical Relevance: With rising cesarean rates, UK obstetricians need evidence-based surgical techniques to prevent long-term complications including abnormal placentation, subfertility, and chronic pelvic pain that burden both patients and NHS resources.
Limitations: Substantial heterogeneity in existing studies regarding techniques, outcome measures, and follow-up prevents definitive surgical recommendations.
PubMed →DOI →
American journal of obstetrics and gynecology

Vacuum-Induced Tamponade Using Urological Catheters for Postpartum Hemorrhage

Ranieri E, Kalimeris S, Ochsenbein N et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Case series
Key Question: Can a novel vacuum tamponade device using urological catheters effectively control postpartum haemorrhage due to uterine atony?
Key Findings:
  • U-CaVIT achieved haemostasis in 17/20 women (85%) with severe PPH (median blood loss 1.8L) after failed first-line treatment
  • Failed in 2/3 unsuccessful cases due to uterine malformations (arcuate and subseptus uteri)
  • Only 3/20 patients required blood transfusion despite significant haemorrhage
Clinical Relevance: Offers UK maternity units a potentially cost-effective second-line intervention for severe atonic PPH using readily available equipment, particularly relevant given recent MBRRACE-UK emphasis on PPH management.
Limitations: Small case series without control group limits generalisability and comparison with established techniques like Bakri balloon or B-Lynch suture.
PubMed →DOI →
American journal of obstetrics and gynecology

A randomized controlled trial comparing remote blood pressure monitoring with office-based blood pressure monitoring for women at high risk of preeclampsia

Rajkumar T, Hennessy A, Shanmugalingam R et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Multicentre randomised controlled non-inferiority trial
Key Question: Can remote blood pressure monitoring safely replace office-based monitoring in pregnant women at high risk of preeclampsia?
Key Findings:
  • No difference in perinatal composite outcome between remote monitoring and usual care (HR 1.0, 95%CI 0.57-1.76; p=0.99)
  • Remote monitoring reduced total antenatal attendances (median 14 vs 16, p<0.01) and hospital admissions for any cause (HR 0.54, 95%CI 0.30-0.97; p=0.04)
  • Women in remote monitoring group filled more antihypertensive prescriptions (median 5 vs 3, p<0.01)
Clinical Relevance: This evidence supports implementing remote blood pressure monitoring for high-risk preeclampsia patients in NHS antenatal services, potentially reducing clinic burden while maintaining safety outcomes and improving medication adherence.
Limitations: Unblinded design may have influenced clinical decision-making and healthcare utilisation patterns.
PubMed →DOI →
American journal of obstetrics and gynecology

EVALUATION OF WEIGHT CHANGE DURING OVARIAN STIMULATION TREATMENT

Elder S, Flannagan K, Romanski P et al. · 2026 Apr 24
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: What is the expected weight change during ovarian stimulation cycles and does this differ in patients with OHSS or multiple cycles?
Key Findings:
  • Mean weight gain was 0.64 ± 1.64 kg (range -2.3 to +6.8 kg) across 22,106 antagonist cycles
  • Lower BMI and higher oocyte yield (≥10) were associated with greater weight gain
  • Patients with OHSS gained 47% more weight than those without (0.94 vs 0.64 kg), though not statistically significant (p=0.08)
Clinical Relevance: Provides evidence-based counselling data for the increasing number of UK patients undergoing IVF or fertility preservation, addressing a common patient concern with reassuring figures showing modest, temporary weight changes.
Limitations: Retrospective design with potential selection bias and missing data on timing of weight return to baseline for all patients.
PubMed →DOI →
American journal of obstetrics and gynecology

Early pregnancy oxylipin markers of inflammation and oxidative stress are associated with small-for-gestational-age birth and specific phenotypes of fetal growth restriction

Ferguson KK, Welch BM, Stevens DR et al. · 2026 Apr 24
Study Type: Case-cohort study
Key Question: Are early pregnancy oxylipin biomarkers of inflammation and oxidative stress associated with small-for-gestational-age births and specific fetal growth restriction phenotypes?
Key Findings:
  • Urinary thromboxane-A2 metabolite at ~10 weeks gestation was associated with SGA birth (OR 1.43, 95% CI 1.20-1.72), with strongest association for late-pregnancy growth restriction phenotype
  • 5-series isoprostane was associated with early-pregnancy growth restriction phenotype (OR 2.22, 95% CI 1.34-3.69) compared to appropriate-for-gestational-age
  • No significant associations found with large-for-gestational-age births after correction for multiple testing
Clinical Relevance: Early pregnancy oxylipin biomarkers may offer potential tools for predicting fetal growth restriction phenotypes, which could inform targeted surveillance and intervention strategies in UK antenatal care.
Limitations: Single timepoint biomarker measurement at 10 weeks may not capture dynamic changes throughout pregnancy that influence fetal growth outcomes.
PubMed →DOI →
BJOG : an international journal of obstetrics and gynaecology

Does Adherence Modify Low-Dose Aspirin Effects in Pregnancy? Post Hoc Analysis From a Multi-Centre Trial

Mwenechanya M, Tembo AM, Chomba E et al. · 2026 Apr 19
Study Type: Post hoc analysis of randomized controlled trial
Key Question: Does adherence level or timing of initiation modify the effectiveness of low-dose aspirin started at 6-13 weeks' gestation for preventing preterm delivery?
Key Findings:
  • Among 11,908 women with 85.5% achieving >90% adherence, neither timing of aspirin initiation (RR 0.97, 95% CI 0.93-1.02 per week delay) nor adherence level (RR 1.01, 95% CI 0.99-1.04 per 5% adherence increase) modified aspirin's effect on preterm delivery
  • Similar null findings for preterm delivery <34 weeks and perinatal mortality across different adherence levels and initiation timing
  • No regional variation in treatment effects across low-resource settings in Africa, Asia, and Latin America
Clinical Relevance: This supports current UK guidance that low-dose aspirin effectiveness for preterm birth prevention remains consistent regardless of precise timing within the 6-13 week window or minor variations in patient adherence.
Limitations: Post hoc analysis design limits causal inferences about adherence-outcome relationships.
PubMed →DOI →
BJOG : an international journal of obstetrics and gynaecology

Immediate Versus Delayed Postpartum and Postabortion Insertion of Long-Acting Reversible Contraception: An Umbrella Review

Zhang Y, Chen Y, Zhang X et al. · 2026 Apr 19
Study Type: Umbrella review (systematic review of systematic reviews and meta-analyses)
Key Question: Does immediate versus delayed long-acting reversible contraception (LARC) insertion after delivery or abortion improve contraceptive uptake and pregnancy prevention?
Key Findings:
  • Immediate LARC insertion increased 6-month utilisation and reduced 6-month pregnancy rates by 84% compared to delayed insertion (RR 0.16, 95% CI 0.04-0.71)
  • Immediate intrauterine device insertion significantly increased expulsion risk, particularly after vaginal delivery (RR 5.09, 95% CI 2.04-12.73) and first-trimester medical abortion (RR 2.02, 95% CI 1.37-2.98)
  • Benefits were observed in both postpartum and postabortion populations, though evidence quality was higher for postpartum settings
Clinical Relevance: This supports immediate LARC insertion policies in UK maternity and abortion services to reduce unintended pregnancy rates, aligning with FSRH and RCOG guidance on improving contraceptive access.
Limitations: Evidence quality was only moderate to low, particularly for postabortion populations, limiting confidence in clinical recommendations.
PubMed →DOI →
BJOG : an international journal of obstetrics and gynaecology

Pregnancy Outcomes Among Individuals With Cerebral Palsy: A Population-Based Cohort Study

Vainder M, Berndl A, Patrikar A et al. · 2026 Apr 22
Study Type: Population-based cohort study
Key Question: What are the maternal and neonatal risks during pregnancy and birth for individuals with cerebral palsy compared to those without?
Key Findings:
  • Women with cerebral palsy had significantly higher risks of severe maternal morbidity/mortality (aRR 1.79, 95% CI 1.36-2.34), caesarean section (aRR 1.38), and hospital readmission (aRR 1.86)
  • Their newborns had increased risks of preterm birth (aRR 1.67), small for gestational age (aRR 1.34), congenital anomalies (aRR 1.77), and severe neonatal morbidity/mortality (aRR 1.48)
  • Peripartum seizure disorders were markedly elevated (aRR 13.06, 95% CI 10.45-16.33)
Clinical Relevance: This large population study provides robust evidence for enhanced preconception counselling and multidisciplinary perinatal care planning for women with cerebral palsy in NHS maternity services.
Limitations: Single-province Canadian data may limit generalisability to UK populations and healthcare systems.
PubMed →DOI →
